Arguments.prompt: Query the USGS Earthquake Hazards Program FDSNWS event API at https://earthquake.usgs.gov/fdsnws/event/1/query with the following parameters: format=geojson, minimum magnitude of 2.0, and a bounding box covering the western United States defined by minimum latitude 32, maximum latitude 49, minimum longitude -125, and maximum longitude -114. Set the start time to exactly 30 days before today's date and the end time to today's date (compute both dates dynamically at runtime using Python's datetime module). Retrieve the full GeoJSON response. Parse the GeoJSON FeatureCollection and extract the following fields from each feature: magnitude (from properties.mag), depth in kilometers (from geometry.coordinates index 2), latitude (from geometry.coordinates index 1), and longitude (from geometry.coordinates index 0). Also record the queried start date and end date as metadata. Assemble all extracted records into a structured dataset. Save the raw extracted dataset as a CSV file named 'earthquake_raw.csv' with columns: longitude, latitude, magnitude, depth. Save the queried start date and end date as a two-line plain text file named 'query_dates.txt', with the start date on the first line and the end date on the second line, both formatted as YYYY-MM-DD.

Load the raw earthquake CSV dataset produced by the previous step. The dataset contains columns: longitude, latitude, magnitude, and depth. Clean the dataset by dropping any rows where magnitude or depth is null or missing. After cleaning, compute a marker size for each earthquake record using the exponential formula: size = 80 * (2 ** (magnitude - min_magnitude)), where min_magnitude is the minimum magnitude value present in the cleaned dataset. Add the computed marker sizes as a new column named 'marker_size'. Save the cleaned and enriched dataset as a CSV file named 'earthquake_processed.csv' with columns: longitude, latitude, magnitude, depth, marker_size. Also save a plain text file named 'event_count.txt' containing a single integer representing the number of earthquake records remaining after filtering.

Download the US Census Bureau cartographic boundary shapefile for US states at the 20-million scale from https://www2.census.gov/geo/tiger/GENZ2018/shp/cb_2018_us_state_20m.zip. Extract and load the shapefile using geopandas. Filter the GeoDataFrame to retain only the following states: California, Oregon, Washington, Nevada, Idaho, Arizona, Utah, and Montana. Match on the NAME field. Save the filtered state boundaries as a GeoJSON file named 'western_states.geojson'.

Load the following inputs produced by previous steps: (1) the processed earthquake CSV dataset with columns longitude, latitude, magnitude, depth, and marker_size; (2) the western US state boundaries GeoJSON file containing polygon geometries for California, Oregon, Washington, Nevada, Idaho, Arizona, Utah, and Montana; (3) the query dates text file containing the start date on the first line and end date on the second line; (4) the event count text file containing the integer count of filtered earthquake events. Using matplotlib, create a figure with a size of 12 by 8 inches. Set both the figure facecolor and the axes facecolor to the dark navy hex color #0d1b2a. Plot the state boundary geometries from the GeoJSON file using geopandas plot or matplotlib Polygon patches, rendering them with no fill, an edge color of light gray (#cccccc), and a line width of 0.8. Overlay a dashed grid on the axes using linestyle '--', alpha 0.3, and line width 0.5, colored light gray. Plot the earthquake scatter points using matplotlib's scatter function with the plasma colormap applied to the depth column, the precomputed marker_size column for sizes, and black edge colors with a line width of 0.3. After plotting, call set_aspect('equal') on the axes to maintain geographic proportions. Set the x-axis limits to -125 and -114, and the y-axis limits to 32 and 49. Add a colorbar to the figure labeled 'Depth (km)' using the scatter plot's mappable. Add a legend positioned outside the map to the right using loc='upper left' and bbox_to_anchor=(1.15, 1.0). The legend should display five representative magnitude entries for M2, M3, M4, M5, and M6, computing their representative sizes using the same exponential formula (size = 80 * (2 ** (magnitude - min_magnitude))) where min_magnitude is the minimum magnitude in the dataset. Style the legend with markerscale=0.4, labelspacing=1.2, and handletextpad=1.0. Set the axes title to a single string formatted as: 'Western US Earthquakes (M2.0+) | <start_date> to <end_date> | <N> events', substituting the actual start date, end date, and event count read from the input files. Set the title color to white so it is visible against the dark background. Set axis tick label colors to white as well. Save the completed figure as a PNG file named 'earthquake_map.png' using savefig with bbox_inches='tight' and dpi=150.
